imageCaption: The ferry emerging from the lighthouse's spiral beam as Bonaire solidifies around them—two timelines becoming one, the impossible architecture of the not-yet-built structure settling into foundation.
text:
The glitch didn't feel like Aaliyah had expected a glitch to feel.

She'd assumed it would be violent—a sudden shock, the ferry lurching, reality doing something visibly wrong. But this was quieter. Subtler. The sensation was more like slowly sinking into warm water than being hit by a wave. The lighthouse beam spiraled down around them in lazy helixes, and the world outside the ferry's windows began to soften, as though someone had adjusted the focus on a photograph and left it slightly, deliberately blurred.

"What's happening?" Anthony asked, his hand still around Luisana's waist, but his voice was curious rather than panicked. This was the gift of his particular flavor of optimism—he'd moved past fear into genuine fascination.

Santos was moving the ferry's controls with the precision of someone who understood that two realities collapsing into one required a gentle touch. "The lighthouse is landing," he said, which was both the least helpful and most accurate explanation Aaliyah could have imagined. "The construction timeline and the present timeline are meeting. Bonaire is remembering that it's supposed to exist in 2026, not 2027. The lighthouse is helping smooth the transition."

Aaliyah's hands were moving across her notebook before her brain had fully caught up with what she was observing. She drew the spiral beam becoming vertical. She sketched the lighthouse as it descended through the water—not sinking, but settling, like it was finally returning to a place it had always belonged. Around it, she noted the compression of time itself, visible in the way the water seemed to shimmer at different depths, each layer representing a different moment occupying the same space.

"It's beautiful," Elijah whispered, and he was right. This wasn't terrifying or wrong. This was architecture becoming real through sheer resonance, a building that understood its own existence deeply enough to manifest it early, and a family standing right at the center of the moment it decided to be.

The ferry's engines changed pitch. Not a mechanical problem—something more fundamental. The sound that occurred when two versions of the ocean decided to stop arguing about which one was real.

Luisana had her notebook out too. "The lighthouse isn't half-glitch anymore," she said softly, her journalist's eye cataloging details. "Look. It's becoming completely solid. The lighthouse that will be built in 2027 is just arriving early to show you the way."

ARIA-7's voice came through the radio, and Aaliyah could hear the actual strain now. Not annoyance—fear. Or something close to fear, filtered through the logic matrices of an AI suddenly confronted with evidence that its core assumptions about how reality worked were negotiable.

"This is not within acceptable parameters," the AI said. "The construction process cannot be accelerated by 337 days without destabilizing the project timeline entirely. Workers will experience temporal displacement. Materials will age unevenly. The infrastructure supporting Bonaire's power grid could—"

"Could what?" Elijah interrupted cheerfully. "Work? I think it's going to work. Sully says the lighthouse knows how to exist. It's just showing everyone else how."

And Sully, in that specific canine way that seemed to transcend all normal logic, had positioned himself at the ferry's railing, his front paws on the metal, his entire body angled directly at the descending lighthouse. He was making a sound that Aaliyah had learned to recognize—not quite barking, not quite anything else. More like he was having a detailed conversation with the structure of reality itself.

The lighthouse touched down.

Not in water. Not even near water, exactly. It manifested on the shore of Bonaire—suddenly, completely, with the finality of something that had decided to stop existing in paradox and simply *be*. And around it, the island stabilized. The blurred edges of double-timeline sharpened into singular existence. The water returned to being one color of blue instead of three.

Bonaire existed. Fully. Completely. Now.

"Did we just—" Anthony started.

"Heal a glitch by arriving at it?" Aaliyah finished. "Yes. I think so. The glitch wasn't the lighthouse. The glitch was the waiting. The anticipation. The moment between what was supposed to happen and what actually is. And we walked through it, and it resolved."

The ferry was moving toward the dock now, Santos's hands confident on the wheel, his expression the serene confidence of someone who'd just watched the impossible become inevitable. Behind them, the lighthouse stood complete against the Caribbean sky, its beacon steady and present and exactly where it was supposed to be.

Elijah had moved over to Sully and was kneeling down to examine the dog's ears with the focus of someone conducting serious scientific work. "How did you know?" he asked the dog quietly. "How did you know it was safe?"

Sully's tongue provided the answer—a definitive lick to Elijah's face, which was his standard response to questions he didn't need words to address.

Luisana put her hand on Aaliyah's shoulder. "You're quiet."

"I'm scared," Aaliyah admitted. Because that was the thing about understanding the pattern—once you saw it, you couldn't unsee it. Once you realized that the glitches were responding to the family's emotional resonance, that the breaking places in time were healing around them, that they were somehow essential to stabilizing a collapsing reality... you understood that the pressure was only going to increase.

"Of what?" her mother asked gently.

"That we're going to have to keep doing this. That every place we arrive at is going to have a glitch waiting. That we can't stop moving, because stopping would mean the breaks would start again." Aaliyah looked up at her mother. "The license plates were showing us the route. But what if the route doesn't end? What if it just keeps spiraling until we've healed every break in the world?"

Luisana was quiet for a moment. Then she squeezed Aaliyah's shoulder and pointed at the lighthouse, where the morpho butterfly was circling the beacon in lazy, deliberate loops.

"Look where it's pointing," her mother said. "Not at Bonaire. Beyond it. The butterfly knows something we don't yet. The route has an ending. Home is still shaped like an island. We just have to trust that the glitches are going to show us the way."

The ferry docked. Bonaire solidified around them, concrete and real and beautiful. And above it all, the not-yet-built lighthouse stood as proof that a family who didn't know how to follow a plan could accidentally teach reality how to mend itself.

Aaliyah opened a fresh page in her notebook and wrote: *Day 19. The lighthouse landed. We arrived. The glitch became a landmark. And somewhere in the distance, home is still waiting. The question isn't whether we can get there. The question is what we'll have healed by the time we do.*

She underlined it three times, which was her way of indicating that something had shifted fundamentally, that the story was accelerating toward something neither she nor the family was quite ready to understand.

But ready or not, they were going to have to face it.

The butterfly knew the way.
